The **romanization of Japanese** is the use of [Latin script](/wiki/Latin_script "Latin script") to write the [Japanese language](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language").[[1]](#cite_note-1) This method of writing is sometimes referred to in Japanese as ***rōmaji*** (ローマ字; lit. 'Roman letters', [[ɾoːma(d)ʑi]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Japanese "Help:IPA/Japanese") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Ja-Romaji.oga "File:Ja-Romaji.oga") or [[ɾoːmaꜜ(d)ʑi]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Japanese "Help:IPA/Japanese")).

Japanese is normally written in a combination of [logographic characters](/wiki/Logogram "Logogram") borrowed from Chinese ([kanji](/wiki/Kanji "Kanji")) and [syllabic](/wiki/Syllabary "Syllabary") scripts ([kana](/wiki/Kana "Kana")) that also ultimately derive from Chinese characters.

There are several different [romanization](/wiki/Romanization "Romanization") systems. The three main ones are [Hepburn romanization](/wiki/Hepburn_romanization "Hepburn romanization"), [Kunrei-shiki romanization](/wiki/Kunrei-shiki_romanization "Kunrei-shiki romanization") (ISO 3602) and [Nihon-shiki romanization](/wiki/Nihon-shiki_romanization "Nihon-shiki romanization") (ISO 3602 Strict). Variants of the Hepburn system are the most widely used.

Romanized Japanese may be used in any context where Japanese text is targeted at non-Japanese speakers who cannot read kanji or kana, such as for names on street signs and passports and in dictionaries and textbooks for foreign learners of the language. It is also used to transliterate Japanese terms in text written in English (or other languages that use the Latin script) on topics related to Japan, such as linguistics, literature, history, and culture.

All Japanese who have attended elementary school since the [Allied occupation](/wiki/Occupation_of_Japan "Occupation of Japan") began in the aftermath of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") have been taught to read and write romanized Japanese.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Therefore, almost all Japanese can read and write Japanese by using *rōmaji*. However, it is extremely rare in Japan to use it to write Japanese (except as an input tool on a computer or for special purposes such as logo design), and most Japanese are more comfortable in reading kanji and kana.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romanization_of_Japanese&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The earliest Japanese romanization system was based on [Portuguese orthography](/wiki/Portuguese_orthography "Portuguese orthography"). It was developed c. 1548 by a Japanese Catholic named [Anjirō](/wiki/Anjir%C5%8D "Anjirō").[[3]](#cite_note-3)[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [Jesuit](/wiki/Society_of_Jesus "Society of Jesus") priests used the system in a series of printed Catholic books so that missionaries could preach and teach their converts without learning to read Japanese orthography. The most useful of these books for the study of early modern Japanese pronunciation and early attempts at romanization was the *[Nippo Jisho](/wiki/Nippo_Jisho "Nippo Jisho")*, a Japanese–Portuguese dictionary written in 1603. In general, the early Portuguese system was similar to Nihon-shiki in its treatment of [vowels](/wiki/Vowel "Vowel"). Some [consonants](/wiki/Consonant "Consonant") were transliterated differently: for instance, the /k/ consonant was rendered, depending on context, as either *c* or *q*, and the /ɸ/ consonant (now pronounced /h/, except before *u*) as *f*; and so *Nihon no kotoba* ("The language of Japan") was spelled *Nifon no cotoba*. The Jesuits also printed some secular books in romanized Japanese, including the first printed edition of the Japanese classic *[The Tale of the Heike](/wiki/The_Tale_of_the_Heike "The Tale of the Heike")*, romanized as *Feiqe no monogatari*, and a collection of *[Aesop's Fables](/wiki/Aesop%27s_Fables "Aesop's Fables")* (romanized as *Esopo no fabulas*). The latter continued to be printed and read after the suppression of [Christianity in Japan](/wiki/Christianity_in_Japan "Christianity in Japan") (Chibbett, 1977).

From the mid-19th century onward, several systems were developed, culminating in the [Hepburn system](/wiki/Hepburn_romanization "Hepburn romanization"), named after [James Curtis Hepburn](/wiki/James_Curtis_Hepburn "James Curtis Hepburn") who used it in the third edition of his Japanese–English dictionary, published in 1887. The Hepburn system included representation of some sounds that have since changed. For example, [Lafcadio Hearn](/wiki/Lafcadio_Hearn "Lafcadio Hearn")'s book *[Kwaidan](/wiki/Kwaidan:_Stories_and_Studies_of_Strange_Things "Kwaidan: Stories and Studies of Strange Things")* shows the older *kw-* pronunciation; in modern Hepburn romanization, this would be written *[Kaidan](/wiki/Kwaidan "Kwaidan")* (lit. 'ghost tales').[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### As a replacement for the Japanese writing system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romanization_of_Japanese&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: As a replacement for the Japanese writing system")]

In the [Meiji era](/wiki/Meiji_era "Meiji era") (1868–1912), some Japanese scholars advocated abolishing the [Japanese writing system](/wiki/Japanese_writing_system "Japanese writing system") entirely and using *rōmaji* instead. The Nihon-shiki romanization was an outgrowth of that movement. Several Japanese texts were published entirely in *rōmaji* during this period, but it failed to catch on. Later, in the early 20th century, some scholars devised syllabary systems with characters derived from Latin (rather like the [Cherokee syllabary](/wiki/Cherokee_syllabary "Cherokee syllabary")) that were even less popular since they were not based on any historical use of the Latin script.

Today, the use of Nihon-shiki for writing Japanese is advocated by the [Oomoto](/wiki/Oomoto "Oomoto") sect[[4]](#cite_note-4) and some independent organizations.[[5]](#cite_note-5) During the [Allied occupation of Japan](/wiki/Allied_occupation_of_Japan "Allied occupation of Japan"), the government of the [Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers](/wiki/Supreme_Commander_for_the_Allied_Powers "Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers") (SCAP) made it official policy to romanize Japanese. However, that policy failed and a more moderate attempt at [Japanese script reform](/wiki/Japanese_script_reform "Japanese script reform") followed.

## Modern systems

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romanization_of_Japanese&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Modern systems")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Toyooka_Station_Sign.jpg/250px-Toyooka_Station_Sign.jpg)](/wiki/File:Toyooka_Station_Sign.jpg)

Old sign from the [JNR](/wiki/JNR "JNR") era at [Toyooka Station](/wiki/Toyooka_Station_(Hy%C5%8Dgo) "Toyooka Station (Hyōgo)") shows inconsistent romanization. Although in principle Hepburn is used, *Kokuhu* is the *kunrei-shiki* form (which would be [*Kokufu*](/wiki/Kokufu_Station "Kokufu Station") in Hepburn).

### Hepburn

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romanization_of_Japanese&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Hepburn")]

Main article: [Hepburn romanization](/wiki/Hepburn_romanization "Hepburn romanization")

*Hepburn romanization* generally follows English [phonology](/wiki/Phonology "Phonology") with [Romance](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages") vowels. It is an intuitive method of showing [Anglophones](/wiki/English_language "English language") the pronunciation of a word in Japanese. It was standardized in the United States as *American National Standard System for the Romanization of Japanese (Modified Hepburn)*, but that status was abolished on October 6, 1994. Hepburn is the most common romanization system in use today, especially in the English-speaking world.

The *Revised Hepburn* system of romanization uses a [macron](/wiki/Macron_(diacritic) "Macron (diacritic)") to indicate some [long vowels](/wiki/Long_vowel "Long vowel") and an [apostrophe](/wiki/Apostrophe "Apostrophe") to note the separation of easily confused [phonemes](/wiki/Phoneme "Phoneme") (usually, syllabic *n* ん from a following naked vowel or semivowel). For example, the name じゅんいちろう is written with the kana characters *ju*-*n*-*i*-*chi*-*ro*-*u*, and romanized as *Jun'ichirō* in Revised Hepburn. Without the apostrophe, it would not be possible to distinguish this correct reading from the incorrect *ju*-*ni*-*chi*-*ro*-*u* (じゅにちろう). This system is widely used in Japan and among foreign students and academics.

### Nihon-shiki

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romanization_of_Japanese&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Nihon-shiki")]

Main article: [Nihon-shiki romanization](/wiki/Nihon-shiki_romanization "Nihon-shiki romanization")

*Nihon-shiki romanization* was originally invented as a method for Japanese to write their own language in Latin characters, rather than to transcribe it for Westerners as Hepburn was. It strictly follows the Japanese syllabary, with no adjustments for changes in pronunciation. It has also been standardized as [ISO 3602 Strict](/wiki/ISO_3602_Strict "ISO 3602 Strict"). Also known as *Nippon-shiki*, rendered in the Nihon-shiki style of romanization the name is either *Nihon-siki* or *Nippon-siki*.

### Kunrei-shiki

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romanization_of_Japanese&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Kunrei-shiki")]

Main article: [Kunrei-shiki romanization](/wiki/Kunrei-shiki_romanization "Kunrei-shiki romanization")

*Kunrei-shiki romanization* is a slightly modified version of Nihon-shiki which eliminates differences between the kana syllabary and modern pronunciation. For example, the characters づ and ず are [pronounced identically](/wiki/Yotsugana "Yotsugana") in modern Japanese, and thus Kunrei-shiki and Hepburn ignore the difference in kana and represent the sound in the same way (*zu*). Nihon-shiki, on the other hand, will romanize づ as *du*, but ず as *zu*. Similarly for the pair じ and ぢ, they are both *zi* in Kunrei-shiki and *ji* in Hepburn, but are *zi* and *di* respectively in Nihon-shiki. See the table below for full details.

Kunrei-shiki has been standardized by the [Japanese Government](/wiki/Japanese_Government "Japanese Government") and the [International Organization for Standardization](/wiki/International_Organization_for_Standardization "International Organization for Standardization") as [ISO 3602](/wiki/ISO_3602 "ISO 3602"). Kunrei-shiki is taught to [Japanese elementary school](/wiki/Education_in_Japan "Education in Japan") students in their fourth year of education.

Written in Kunrei-shiki, the name of the system would be rendered *Kunreisiki*.

### Other variants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romanization_of_Japanese&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Other variants")]

It is possible to elaborate these romanizations to enable non-native speakers to pronounce Japanese words more correctly. Typical additions include [tone](/wiki/Tone_(linguistics) "Tone (linguistics)") marks to note the [Japanese pitch accent](/wiki/Japanese_pitch_accent "Japanese pitch accent") and [diacritic marks](/wiki/Diacritic_mark "Diacritic mark") to distinguish phonological changes, such as the assimilation of the moraic nasal /ɴ/ (see [Japanese phonology](/wiki/Japanese_phonology "Japanese phonology")).

### JSL

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romanization_of_Japanese&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: JSL")]

Main article: [JSL romanization](/wiki/JSL_romanization "JSL romanization")

*JSL* is a romanization system based on Japanese phonology, designed using the linguistic principles used by linguists in designing writing systems for languages that do not have any. It is a purely [phonemic](/wiki/Phoneme "Phoneme") system, using exactly one symbol for each phoneme, and marking [the pitch accent](/wiki/Japanese_pitch_accent "Japanese pitch accent") using [diacritics](/wiki/Diacritic "Diacritic"). It was created for [Eleanor Harz Jorden's](/wiki/Eleanor_Jorden "Eleanor Jorden") system of Japanese language teaching. Its principle is that such a system enables students to internalize the phonology of Japanese better. Since it does not have any of the other systems' advantages for non-native speakers, and the Japanese already have a writing system for their language, JSL is not widely used outside the educational environment.

## Non-standard romanization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romanization_of_Japanese&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Non-standard romanization")]

In addition to the standardized systems above, there are many variations in romanization, used either for simplification, in error or confusion between different systems, or for deliberate stylistic reasons.

Notably, the various mappings that [Japanese input methods](/wiki/Japanese_input_methods "Japanese input methods") use to convert keystrokes on a Roman keyboard to kana often combine features of all of the systems; when used as plain text rather than being converted, these are usually known as *[wāpuro rōmaji](/wiki/W%C4%81puro_r%C5%8Dmaji "Wāpuro rōmaji")*. (*Wāpuro* is a [blend](/wiki/Blend_word "Blend word") of *wādo* *purosessā* [word processor](/wiki/Word_processor "Word processor").) Unlike the standard systems, *wāpuro rōmaji* requires no characters from outside the [ASCII](/wiki/ASCII "ASCII") character set.

While there may be arguments in favour of some of these variant romanizations in specific contexts, their use, especially if mixed, leads to confusion when romanized Japanese words are indexed. This confusion never occurs when inputting Japanese characters with a word processor, because input Latin letters are transliterated into Japanese kana as soon as the [IME](/wiki/Input_Method_Editor "Input Method Editor") processes what character is input.

### Dzu

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romanization_of_Japanese&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Dzu")]

A common practice is to romanize づ as *dzu*, allowing to distinguish it from both ドゥ *du* and ず *zu*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] For example, it can be seen in the style of romanization [Google Translate](/wiki/Google_Translate "Google Translate") adheres to. It is not to be conflated with the older form of Hepburn romanization, which used *dzu* for both ず and づ.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

### Long vowels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romanization_of_Japanese&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Long vowels")]

In addition, the following three "non-Hepburn *rōmaji*" (非ヘボン式ローマ字, *hi-Hebon-shiki rōmaji*) methods of representing long vowels are authorized by the Japanese Foreign Ministry for use in passports.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

* *oh* for おお or おう (Hepburn *ō*).
* *oo* for おお or おう. This is valid JSL romanization. For Hepburn romanization, it is not a valid romanization if the long vowel belongs within a single word.
* *ou* for おう. This is also an example of *[wāpuro rōmaji](/wiki/W%C4%81puro_r%C5%8Dmaji "Wāpuro rōmaji")*.

## Spacing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romanization_of_Japanese&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Spacing")]

Japanese is written without spaces between words, and in some cases, such as compounds, it may not be completely clear where word boundaries should lie, resulting in varying romanization styles. For example, 結婚する, meaning "to marry", and composed of the noun 結婚 (*kekkon*, "marriage") combined with する (*suru*, "to do"), is romanized as one word *kekkonsuru* by some authors but two words *kekkon suru* by others. [Particles](/wiki/Japanese_particles "Japanese particles"), like the possessive particle [の](/wiki/%E3%81%AE "の") in 君の犬 ("your dog"), are sometimes joined with the preceding term (*kimino inu*), or written as separate words (*kimi no inu*).

## Kana without standardized forms of romanization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romanization_of_Japanese&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Kana without standardized forms of romanization")]

There is no universally accepted style of romanization for the smaller versions of the vowels and *y*-row kana when used outside the normal combinations (きゃ, きょ, ファ etc.), nor for the [sokuon](/wiki/Sokuon "Sokuon") or small *tsu* kana っ/ッ when it is not directly followed by a consonant. Although these are usually regarded as merely phonetic marks or diacritics, they do sometimes appear on their own, such as at the end of sentences, in exclamations, or in some names. The detached sokuon, representing a final glottal stop in exclamations, is sometimes represented as an apostrophe or as *t*; for example, あっ! might be written as *a'!* or *at!*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Roman letter names in Japanese

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Romanization_of_Japanese&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Roman letter names in Japanese")]

The list below shows the Japanese readings of letters in Katakana, for spelling out words, or in acronyms. For example, [NHK](/wiki/NHK "NHK") is read *enu-eichi-kē* (エヌ・エイチ・ケー). These are the standard names, based on the British English letter names (so Z is from *zed*, not *zee*), but in specialized circumstances, names from other languages may also be used. For example, musical keys are often referred to by the German names, so that B♭ is called *bē* (べー) from German B (German: [[beː]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German")).

* A; *ē* (エー, sometimes pronounced *ei*, エイ)
* B; *bī* (ビー)
* C; *shī* (シー, sometimes pronounced *sī*, スィー)
* D; *dī* (ディー, sometimes pronounced *dē*, デー)
* E; *ī* (イー)
* F; *efu* (エフ)
* G; *jī* (ジー)
* H; *eichi* or *etchi* (エイチ or エッチ)
* I; *ai* (アイ)
* J; *jē* (ジェー, sometimes pronounced *jei*, ジェイ)
* K; *kē* (ケー, sometimes pronounced *kei*, ケイ)
* L; *eru* (エル)
* M; *emu* (エム)
* N; *enu* (エヌ)
* O; *ō* (オー)
* P; *pī* (ピー)
* Q; *kyū* (キュー)
* R; *āru* (アール)
* S; *esu* (エス)
* T; *tī* (ティー)
* U; *yū* (ユー)
* V; *bui* or *vī* (ブイ or ヴィー)
* W; *daburyū* (ダブリュー)
* X; *ekkusu* (エックス)
* Y; *wai* (ワイ)
* Z; *zetto* (ゼット)

Sources: *[Kōjien](/wiki/K%C5%8Djien "Kōjien")* (7th edition), [Daijisen](/wiki/Daijisen "Daijisen") (online version).[*[full citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#What_information_to_include "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] Daijisen does not mention the name *vī*, while Kōjien does.
